title: The restorer who leaves the seams visible
summary: A Bologna painting restorer has spent thirty years repairing damaged altarpieces, and she refuses to hide her own work.
body:
Chiara Venturi works in a small studio near Piazza Maggiore in Bologna. She has restored paintings for over thirty years. Most of her clients are small parish churches that own one or two old altarpieces and cannot afford a museum-grade restorer from Rome. Chiara is, by reputation, careful and slow.

What makes her work unusual is a decision she made early in her career. When she repairs a section of a painting that has been lost — a corner that flaked off, a face damaged by candle smoke, a hand scratched away in a nineteenth-century cleaning — she does not pretend the damage was never there. From a metre away the painting looks whole. From thirty centimetres, however, the restored area is visibly different: a finer brush, a colour close but not exact, a thin line of bare canvas left around the edge. She calls this her seam.

Many colleagues disagree. The traditional view, especially in Italy, is that a good restoration is invisible — the restorer's hand should disappear into the original. Chiara argues the opposite. A painting that has survived four hundred years has, in her view, a biography, and the damage is part of that biography. To hide the repair, she says, is to lie about the painting's life. To leave a faint seam is to tell the viewer: this part was lost, and someone in 2007 chose to fill it in.

She has lost some commissions because of this. A wealthy private collector in Milan, who hired her in 2014 to repair a small seventeenth-century portrait, refused the finished work and demanded that another restorer 'finish properly'. Chiara returned the fee. Several churches, however, have come to her precisely because of the method. A priest in a village outside Ravenna told her he wanted his congregation to see that the painting had been hurt and helped, the same way the building itself had been.

A conservation student from Florence interviewed her last year for a thesis. The student asked whether the seam was a kind of signature — a way of leaving her mark on every painting. Chiara said no. The seam, she said, is not her signature. It is the painting's wound, kept open just enough that anyone who looks closely can still see it. Her name appears nowhere on the canvas. It appears only in the small report she leaves with the church, which lists what was damaged, what she did, and what she chose not to do.

Last spring, although she is past the usual retirement age, Chiara took on a difficult panel from a Franciscan church near Imola. A whole saint's face had been lost. She has worked on it for nine months. The face will be there again by Easter. From across the nave, it will look complete. From close up, a thin pale line will run along the jaw — quiet, deliberate, and easy to miss unless you have been told to look for it.

annotations.meaningVi: dấu tay của người phục chế
tiểu sử / lịch sử cuộc đời

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for life-experience and ongoing work
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng present perfect (have/has + V3) cho kinh nghiệm hoặc việc bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn liên quan đến hiện tại. Trong bài: 'She has restored paintings for over thirty years.' (bắt đầu hơn 30 năm trước, vẫn đang làm). 'Several churches... have come to her precisely because of the method.' (đã đến — kết quả còn giá trị). 'She has worked on it for nine months.' (vẫn đang làm). 'A painting that has survived four hundred years...' (đã tồn tại đến nay). So sánh: 'She made a decision early in her career' (past simple — sự kiện đã xong, không nhấn mạnh liên kết hiện tại).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses present perfect (have/has + V3) for experience or actions that started in the past and still matter now. In the text: 'She has restored paintings for over thirty years.' (still doing it). 'Several churches... have come to her.' (and still come). 'She has worked on it for nine months.' (still working). 'A painting that has survived four hundred years...' (still here). Compare 'She made a decision early in her career' (past simple — finished event, no link to now).

translationVi:
Chiara Venturi làm việc trong một xưởng nhỏ ở tầng ba của một toà nhà gần Quảng trường Maggiore ở Bologna. Bà đã phục chế tranh hơn ba mươi năm. Hầu hết khách hàng là các nhà thờ giáo xứ nhỏ có một hai bức tranh bàn thờ cổ và không đủ tiền thuê người phục chế đạt chuẩn bảo tàng từ Rome. Theo tiếng tăm, Chiara cẩn thận và chậm rãi.

Điều khiến công việc của bà khác thường là một quyết định bà đưa ra từ đầu sự nghiệp. Khi bà sửa một mảng tranh đã mất — một góc bong ra, một khuôn mặt bị khói nến làm hỏng, một bàn tay bị cạo mất trong lần lau dọn thế kỷ XIX — bà không giả vờ vết hỏng chưa bao giờ tồn tại. Từ một mét, bức tranh trông nguyên vẹn. Từ ba mươi centimet, vùng được sửa nhìn rõ là hơi khác: cọ mảnh hơn một chút, màu gần đúng nhưng không khớp, một đường vải nguyên mảnh để chừa ở rìa. Bà gọi đó là 'đường nối' của bà.

Nhiều đồng nghiệp không đồng tình. Quan điểm truyền thống, nhất là ở Ý, là một bản phục chế tốt phải vô hình — dấu tay người phục chế phải tan biến vào bản gốc. Chiara lập luận ngược lại. Một bức tranh đã sống bốn trăm năm, theo bà, có một lịch sử cuộc đời, và vết hỏng là một phần của lịch sử đó. Che giấu phần sửa, bà nói, là nói dối về cuộc đời bức tranh. Để lại đường nối mờ là báo cho người xem: phần này từng mất, và năm 2007 ai đó đã chọn lấp nó vào.

Bà đã mất một số hợp đồng vì điều này. Một nhà sưu tập tư nhân giàu có ở Milan, người thuê bà năm 2014 sửa một bức chân dung thế kỷ XVII, đã từ chối thành phẩm và yêu cầu người khác 'làm cho xong cho đàng hoàng'. Chiara trả lại tiền công. Nhưng nhiều nhà thờ tìm đến bà chính vì phương pháp này. Một linh mục ở một làng ngoài Ravenna nói ông muốn giáo dân thấy rằng bức tranh đã bị tổn thương và được chữa, giống như chính ngôi nhà thờ đã bị và được.

Một sinh viên bảo tồn ở Florence phỏng vấn bà năm ngoái cho luận văn. Sinh viên hỏi đường nối có phải là một dạng chữ ký không — cách bà để lại dấu ấn lên mọi bức tranh bà chạm vào. Chiara nói không. Đường nối, bà nói, không phải chữ ký của bà. Đó là vết thương của bức tranh, giữ mở vừa đủ để ai nhìn gần có thể vẫn thấy. Tên bà không nằm trên bức tranh. Nó chỉ nằm trong báo cáo nhỏ bà để lại cho nhà thờ, liệt kê những gì hỏng, bà đã làm gì, và bà chọn không làm gì.

Mùa xuân vừa rồi, dù đã quá tuổi nghỉ hưu thông thường, Chiara nhận một tấm gỗ khó từ một nhà thờ Phanxicô gần Imola. Cả khuôn mặt một vị thánh đã mất. Bà đã làm việc trên nó chín tháng. Khuôn mặt sẽ hiện diện trở lại trước lễ Phục Sinh. Từ phía bên kia gian giữa, nó sẽ trông trọn vẹn. Nhìn sát, một đường nhạt mảnh sẽ chạy dọc theo hàm — yên, có chủ ý, dễ bỏ qua trừ khi đã được dặn nhìn.

options: Third floor of a building near Piazza Maggiore in Bologna

questions.prompt: Who are most of her clients?
options: Small parish churches that own one or two old altarpieces and cannot afford a museum-grade restorer from Rome

questions.prompt: What is unusual about her work?
options: She leaves a visible 'seam' — restored areas look slightly different from 30 cm: finer brush, colour close but not exact, a thin line of bare canvas at the edge
She paints over the whole picture
She uses only black and white

questions.prompt: What is the traditional Italian view of restoration?
options: A good restoration is invisible — the restorer's hand should disappear into the original

questions.prompt: How does Chiara justify the seam?
options: A 400-year-old painting has a biography; damage is part of it. Hiding the repair lies about the painting's life; a faint seam tells the viewer the part was lost and someone in 2007 chose to fill it in.

questions.prompt: What happened with the Milan collector in 2014?
options: He refused the finished portrait and demanded another restorer 'finish properly'; Chiara returned the fee

questions.prompt: Why did the priest near Ravenna hire her?
options: He wanted his congregation to see the painting had been hurt and helped, the same way the building itself had been

questions.prompt: How did Chiara answer the student's 'signature' question?
options: No. The seam is the painting's wound, kept open just enough that anyone who looks closely can still see it. Her name appears nowhere on the canvas — only in the report she leaves with the church.
Yes, the seam is her signature

questions.prompt: What is in the report she leaves with the church?
options: What was damaged, what she did, and what she chose not to do

questions.prompt: What is she working on now?
options: A difficult Franciscan panel near Imola — a whole saint's face was lost; she has worked on it for nine months and will finish before Easter

questions.prompt: How will the finished saint look?
options: From across the nave, complete; from close up, a thin pale line will run along the jaw — quiet, deliberate, easy to miss unless you have been told to look

A restoration can be honest about its own work: by leaving the repair faintly visible, the restorer tells the truth about what a painting has lived through, even though the tradition prefers invisibility.
Old paintings should not be repaired.

From a metre away the painting looks whole. Many colleagues disagree. The traditional view, especially in Italy, is that a good restoration is invisible — the restorer's hand should disappear into the original. Chiara argues the opposite. A painting that has survived four hundred years has, in her view, a {{b4}}, and the damage is part of that biography. To hide the repair, she says, is to lie about the painting's life. To leave a faint seam is to tell the viewer: this part was lost, and someone in 2007 chose to fill it in.

She has lost some commissions because of this. A wealthy private collector in Milan, who hired her in 2014 to repair a small seventeenth-century portrait, refused the finished work and demanded that another restorer 'finish properly'. Chiara returned the {{b5}}. Several churches, however, have come to her precisely because of the method. A priest in a village outside Ravenna told her he wanted his {{b6}} to see that the painting had been hurt and helped, the same way the building itself had been.

A conservation student from Florence interviewed her last year for a {{b7}}. The student asked whether the seam was a kind of signature — a way of leaving her mark on every painting. Chiara said no. The seam, she said, is not her signature. It is the painting's {{b8}}, kept open just enough that anyone who looks closely can still see it. Her name appears nowhere on the canvas. Last spring, although she is past the usual retirement age, Chiara took on a difficult panel from a {{b9}} church near Imola. A whole saint's face had been lost. She has worked on it for nine months. The face will be there again by {{b10}}. From across the nave, it will look complete. From close up, a thin pale line will run along the {{b11}} — quiet, deliberate, and easy to miss unless you have been told to look for it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Chiara believes a repair should stay slightly visible so the painting's history is not erased. In your own life — a scar, a mended jacket, a corrected mistake at work — when is it better to hide the repair, and when is it better to let it show?
